chunk_summary: Lifestyle changes are the first-line treatment for pediatric MASLD, focusing on diet, physical activity, and monitoring ALT levels. Pharmacotherapy, including Vitamin E and GLP-1 receptor agonists, is reserved for specialist-led cases after failed lifestyle interventions.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of lifestyle modifications in managing pediatric MASLD.
Learn the recommended monitoring intervals for ALT and metabolic markers in children with MASLD.
Identify when to consider pharmacotherapy, including Vitamin E and GLP-1 receptor agonists, in pediatric MASLD management.
key_facts: Lifestyle changes include eliminating sugar-sweetened beverages, reducing refined carbohydrates, increasing dietary fiber and protein, and engaging in daily moderate-to-vigorous physical activity.
Screening ALT should be rechecked every 3–6 months if elevated, or every 2–3 years if normal but risk factors persist.
Vitamin E (800 IU daily) has evidence for use in biopsy-proven pediatric steatohepatitis but requires hepatology supervision due to safety concerns.
GLP-1 receptor agonists are considered for anti-obesity therapy in selected cases under specialist care.
Metformin improves insulin sensitivity but is not a liver-directed therapy.
reference_quote: Both the 2017 NASPGHAN and 2024 ISPGHAN guidance emphasize lifestyle change as first-line therapy for pediatric MASLD.
